name: Earle Warren
abstract: Earle Warren (born Earl Ronald Warren; July 1, 1914 – June 4, 1994) was an American saxophonist. He was part of the Count Basie Orchestra from 1937.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Count Basie and His Orchestra with Benny Carter and The Swing Masters: Swing Lives
Newport Jazz Festival: Count Basie and His Orchestra with Benny Carter and The Swing Masters: Swing Lives
New York Jazz Repertory Company: 52nd Street Revisited
New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Count Basie
Newport Jazz Festival: Tribute to Lionel Hampton
Kool Jazz Festival: A Salute to Count Basie

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Schaap
about_quotes.evidence: And then Earle Warren led the reeds. And he was the one I was closest to, because he was the leader and he was a teacher and he was a father.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Schaap interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: After he left Basie’s band he came back to Ohio and formed a band of his own and hired me as a kid to play with him for a few months.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Levy
about_quotes.evidence: I worked with – Earle Warren had a band that worked in a club that Joe Louis fronted. That was with Earle Warren’s band.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Levy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Arnett Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: Earle Warren on saxophone, ie Chamblee Eddy Chorney on saxophone and yours truly, Arnette Cobb.

about_quotes.speaker: Buddy Tate
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER: ...this is when they had the berth that you could sleep in. And then you’d have, every morning the wives with the kids of all the musicians would come and we’d come to breakfast. And there was Snooky, played the trumpet with Basie at the time, and then Earle Warren...
about_quotes.doc_title: Buddy Tate interviewed by Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Doc Cheatham
about_quotes.evidence: They had Earle Warren leading and he was taking the lead, and he was leading the band. And he wasn’t in Count Basie’s band as the leader, see? But he couldn’t make it. He’s too loud and he was excited and he got upset all the time at things, and you can’t do that.
about_quotes.doc_title: Doc Cheatham interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 28, 1995
